nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found on outer and seaward reef faces, typically at depths of 20–100 m. Tends to congregate in mid-water aggregations above coral structure and along reef drop-offs.

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild, feeding on suspended zooplankton above the reef. In captivity tends to accept a wide variety of meaty preparations (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, calanus, cyclopeeze, rotifers), herbivore flakes, and micro-pellets. Multiple small feedings daily are generally advised to match the species' natural grazing pattern. Vitamin- and fatty-acid-enriched foods have been reported to help maintain the juvenile yellow/lavender coloration.

summary: A peaceful Caribbean damselfish whose juveniles display a striking yellow back and lavender body, fading to olive in adults. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly when kept in groups with ample swimming space.
careNotes:
The Sunshine Chromis (Chromis insolata) ranges across the tropical Western Atlantic, including southern Florida, Bermuda, the Bahamas, and the Caribbean Sea. In the wild it tends to occur on deeper outer-reef faces at roughly 20–100 m depth, congregating in mid-water aggregations above coral structure.

Color phase: Juveniles are vivid yellow across the dorsal surface with lavender or purple along the mid-flank and a pearly white belly — the form sold in the aquarium trade as the 'Purple & Yellow' or 'Sunshine' Chromis. As individuals mature, the yellow tends to transition to olive-green above the lateral midline, which is why adult specimens are sometimes marketed separately as the 'Olive Chromis'. Adults generally reach 13–16 cm; specimens kept in undersized tanks have been reported to stay closer to the lower end.

Social dynamics: The Sunshine Chromis is generally considered one of the more peaceful damselfishes, commonly described as peaceful or relatively passive in mixed-species reef systems. In mixed-damsel settings it may show some semi-aggressive behavior toward close relatives. Keeping groups of six or more has been reported to distribute minor intra-group friction more broadly and reduce harassment of weaker individuals; a lone specimen or undersized group can become a target for dominant tankmates.

Disease note: Chromis species, including the Sunshine Chromis, can be susceptible to Uronema marinum and other protozoan infections during shipping and acclimation. An extended quarantine period before introduction to the display tank is generally advised.

Reproduction: Like other Chromis species, the Sunshine Chromis is oviparous, with distinct pairing during spawning. Eggs are demersal and adhesive; the male guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Captive breeding has rarely been reported, and rearing larvae in a home aquarium is considered impractical for most hobbyists.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning. Demersal eggs adhere to a hard substrate cleaned by the male, who then guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Larvae are pelagic and very small; captive rearing has rarely been reported. Breeding in home aquariums is considered difficult and not commonly attempted.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe (yes), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, min tank 30 gal, max size 6.5 in. The catalog collection page at /collections/chromis was used as the navigation entry point per the dispatch prompt.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae; Ovalentaria incertae sedis), max size (16.0 cm TL), native range (Western Atlantic, Florida to Caribbean), depth (20–100 m), temperature (preferred 20–27.5°C, mean 24.6), diet (planktivorous), reproductive biology (oviparous, distinct pairing, demersal adhesive eggs, male parental care). IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: KP Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (beginner), temperament (peaceful), max size (~6.3 in), min tank (30 gal), schooling behavior (groups of 6–7), and the juvenile-to-adult color transition from yellow to olive-green (basis for the 'Olive Chromis' trade name).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), recommends 55 gal minimum for a group (basis for the 200 L tank decision), rated semi-aggressive in mixed-damsel context. Flags Uronema marinum susceptibility.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World (Green Chromis guide, comparative)
additional.notes: Cross-referenced for Chromis genus-level husbandry norms: schooling group of 6+, ample open swimming space, omnivorous diet with frozen meaty foods. Used as a fourth-source sanity check on the 55-gal recommendation for grouped Chromis.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous, with distinct pairing during spawning. Demersal, adhesive eggs are laid on a hard substrate and guarded and aerated by the male until hatching. Captive rearing of larvae has rarely been reported and is generally considered impractical for most hobbyists.
